turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett has the opinion of the court this morning in case 23-715 Advocate Christ Medical Center v. Kennedy.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: This case required the court to interpret a highly technical provision of the Medicare statute. So please bear with my use of some Medicare lingo, and I promise that I will not get too deep into the weeds. When hospitals provide in-patient services to Medicare patients, the Medicare program pays these hospitals a fixed rate for treating each patient. Congress has also created various hospital-specific rate adjustments that provide additional increased funding. And the relevant adjustment in this case is the so-called disproportionate share adjustment, which I am just going to call the adjustment, and this adjustment provides additional funding to hospitals that treat an unusually high percentage of poor Medicare patients. The basic idea is that the poor generally cost more to treat, so hospitals treating an unusually high number of poor patients get extra funding to help cover the costs. The amount of this adjustment depends in part on how many of the hospital's patients are, and this is a quote from the statute, entitled to supplementary security income benefits. Supplementary Security Income (SSI) is a benefit that provides monthly payments to older individuals or those with disabilities who have very low incomes. The plaintiffs in this case are over 200 hospitals that provide medical services to Medicare patients and the hospitals argue that the Department of Health and Human Services has misinterpreted the phrase entitled to SSI benefits under subchapter XVI. According to the hospitals, this phrase encompasses all patients who are enrolled in the SSI system at the time of their hospitalizations, even if the patients were not entitled to an SSI payment during the month of their hospitalization. The hospital's approach would sweep more people into the numerator of the Medicare fraction that determines the amount of money they get thereby increasing the hospital's funding. HHS for its part, interprets the phrase to refer to patients who were entitled to receive an SSI payment during the month of their hospitalization. HHS' approach would sweep fewer people into the numerator of this fraction thereby decreasing the hospital's funding. So the hospitals sued the Secretary of HHS arguing that HHS' interpretation had the effect of underfunding them. We agree with HHS as has every court to consider this issue. SSI benefits in subchapter XVI are about cash payments for needy individuals. It makes little sense as the DC circuit put it to say that individuals are entitled to a benefit in months where they're not eligible to receive it. Thus, for purposes of the Medicare fraction, we hold that an individual is entitled to SSI benefits when she is eligible to receive an SSI cash payment during the month of her hospitalization. I think I can safely assume that you would prefer that I not march the statutory analysis that led us to this conclusion, so suffice it to say that our holding is granted in the statute's text and structure as well as our precedent. Justice Jackson has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Sotomayor joins.
